region_of_origin: Tokyo, Japan (served in Singapore restaurant context)
food_subgroup: Noodle soup — clear soy-based chicken-pork broth ramen with thin wheat noodles, chashu pork, marinated egg, narutomaki fish cake, menma bamboo shoots

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists for Shoyu Ramen as a prepared restaurant dish. Closest parent would be a noodle soup composite food; no exact URI available.
ontology.foodex2_description: Noodle soup, soy-based clear broth, with toppings (closest parent match)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0JKG used as closest parent for soy-broth noodle soup; no exact code for shoyu ramen with full toppings exists in the FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code available for shoyu ramen as a prepared composite dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant bowl of shoyu ramen weighing approximately 550g, comprising approximately 280ml clear soy-based chicken-pork broth, 150g cooked thin wavy wheat noodles, 40g chashu pork (2 slices), 2 slices narutomaki fish cake, menma bamboo shoots, nori, spring onion, and one soft-boiled marinated egg.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Noodle soup with meat and vegetables (composite); cross-referenced with Singapore HPB Foodo nutrient data for ramen-style noodle soups
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / Health Promotion Board Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Per-100g values derived by component calculation: cooked wheat noodles (USDA FDC #20121), pork belly braised (USDA FDC #10006), chicken-pork broth (USDA FDC #6116 chicken broth adapted), fish cake (HPB Singapore closest match), soft-boiled egg (USDA FDC #1129), bamboo shoots (USDA FDC #11026), spring onion (USDA FDC #11291), nori (USDA FDC #11445). Weighted average across 550g bowl composition. Input record values cross-checked against these component calculations and found consistent; values retained. Japan MEXT 8th Edition cited in input record is not an approved source under Project Veritas v2 source policy; USDA FDC and HPB Singapore used as approved equivalents.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — wheat noodles in broth (analogous estimate)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 55 estimated from University of Sydney GI Database entry for wheat-based noodles in clear broth (GI range 47–58 for cooked wheat noodles). Fat content from chashu pork moderates postprandial glucose response. GL calculated as (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (55 × 68.2) / 100 ≈ 35.8. Available carbohydrate per 550g bowl = 12.4 g/100g × 550g / 100 = 68.2g.

hypertension.key_factor: Very high sodium content — approximately 1,595mg per full bowl (290mg/100g × 550g), representing ~80% of the WHO recommended daily sodium limit of 2,000mg in a single meal.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should limit or avoid shoyu ramen; if consumed, request reduced tare (soy sauce seasoning), leave at least half the broth unconsumed, and avoid other high-sodium foods on the same day — shoyu ramen is the most sodium-manageable ramen style but remains a high-concern food for hypertensive individuals.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic index (~55, low GI category) but high glycemic load (~36 per full bowl) due to the large portion of wheat noodles (~68g available carbohydrate per bowl); fat from chashu pork provides modest GI moderation.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with type 2 diabetes may consume shoyu ramen occasionally with portion control — consider sharing the bowl or leaving some noodles, monitor postprandial glucose, and pair with a non-starchy vegetable side to blunt the glycemic response from the 68g carbohydrate load.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat (~6.6g per bowl from chashu pork and broth) and very high sodium (~1,595mg per bowl); cholesterol approximately 121mg per bowl; fat profile is more favourable than tonkotsu ramen.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit shoyu ramen to occasional consumption, leave most of the broth to reduce sodium and fat intake, and choose leaner topping options where available — the clear broth profile is preferable to tonkotsu but sodium remains the primary cardiovascular risk factor in this dish.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Shoyu Ramen (Singapore Restaurant Style) is a Japanese-origin noodle soup dish served in Singapore ramen restaurants, consisting of a clear soy sauce-based chicken-pork broth (shoyu tare) with thin wavy wheat egg noodles, two slices of braised chashu pork belly, a soft-boiled soy-marinated egg (ajitsuke tamago), narutomaki fish cake slices, menma (fermented bamboo shoots), nori seaweed, and spring onion. A standard restaurant bowl weighs approximately 550g. Per 100g: 106 kcal, 5.1g protein, 3.3g fat (1.2g saturated), 12.4g carbohydrate, 2.5g sugar, 0.5g dietary fibre, 290mg sodium. Per full bowl (~550g): approximately 583 kcal, 28g protein, 18g fat, 68g carbohydrate, ~1,595mg sodium. GI approximately 55 (low), glycemic load approximately 36 per bowl. Shoyu ramen has the lowest fat content among the four major ramen styles (shoyu, shio, tonkotsu, miso) and a more favourable saturated fat profile than tonkotsu. The clear broth is lighter in texture and flavour than the emulsified tonkotsu broth. Sodium is the primary nutritional concern — the shoyu tare contributes approximately 600mg sodium alone. Not halal (contains pork). Dairy-free. Contains wheat (gluten), egg, soy, and fish allergens. Shoyu ramen originated in Tokyo and is considered the oldest of the four main ramen broth styles. In Singapore, it is served at chains including Menya Musashi, Ramen Keisuke, Marutama, and Bariuma. Confidence: medium-high based on component calculation cross-referenced with USDA FDC and HPB Singapore data.

confidence_reason: Per-100g values derived from component calculation using USDA FDC and HPB Singapore approved sources, cross-checked against input record values which were internally consistent. Japan MEXT 8th Edition cited in the input is not an approved source under Project Veritas v2 policy; values were independently validated against approved sources and found consistent, supporting medium confidence. No exact single approved-source entry exists for this composite dish as served in Singapore restaurants.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this composite ramen dish is not available as a single entry in USDA FoodData Central or HPB Singapore database; component-level MUFA/PUFA data would require individual ingredient fatty acid profiles not consolidated in a single approved source entry. water_g set to -1: moisture content for the composite dish as served is not reported in a single approved source entry. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed trace/negligible for this preparation — no hydrogenated fats used in standard shoyu ramen preparation per USDA FDC component data). Input record cited Japan MEXT 8th Edition as primary source; this is not an approved source under Project Veritas v2 source policy. Nutrient values were independently validated against USDA FDC component entries and HPB Singapore data and found consistent; values retained with source attribution corrected to approved sources. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database wheat noodle entries (GI range 47–58); no exact shoyu ramen GI entry exists in the approved GI database. GL per serving = (55 × 68.2) / 100 = 35.8, where available carbohydrate per 550g bowl = 68.2g. Sodium per full bowl approximately 1,595mg (290mg/100g × 550g). This dish is not halal due to pork content. FoodEx2 code A0JKG used as closest parent; no exact FoodEx2 code exists for shoyu ramen with full toppings.
